card.title: The Fermi Paradox
card.teaser: The universe is 13.8 billion years old and has trillions of stars. So where is everybody?
card.coverImagePrompt: A lone physicist silhouetted against a vast starfield, looking up at an impossibly silent Milky Way arching overhead, cinematic, awe-inducing, deep blues and violets

lessons.title: A question over lunch
lessons.body: In the summer of 1950, physicist Enrico Fermi was having lunch at Los Alamos with a few colleagues. They were joking about a recent cartoon of aliens stealing trash cans. Fermi went quiet, did some math in his head, and then blurted out: 'But where is everybody?' That offhand question became one of the most haunting puzzles in science.

visual.imagePrompt: Four physicists at a 1950s lunch table, one mid-sentence, chalkboard equations visible behind them, warm nostalgic lighting

lessons.title: The numbers don't add up
lessons.body: The Milky Way alone contains roughly 400 billion stars, and we now know most of them have planets. Even if only a tiny fraction develop life, and a tiny fraction of that develops intelligence, the galaxy should be teeming with civilizations — many of them billions of years older than us. A single civilization spreading at even 1% of lightspeed could colonize the galaxy in a few million years.

keyTerms.definition: A formula estimating the number of communicative civilizations in the galaxy
The contradiction between high probability of alien life and the total lack of evidence for it

visual.imagePrompt: Chalkboard filled with the Drake Equation and orbital diagrams, a single cup of coffee steaming beside it

lessons.title: The Great Filter
lessons.body: One chilling idea is the Great Filter: somewhere on the path from lifeless chemistry to galaxy-spanning civilization, there is a barrier almost nothing gets past. The scary part is we don't know if the filter is behind us — meaning life itself is a fluke — or ahead of us, meaning advanced civilizations tend to self-destruct before going interstellar.
lessons.funFact: Philosopher Nick Bostrom has argued that finding fossil microbes on Mars would actually be terrible news: it would mean life is common, and the filter is probably still waiting for us.

visual.imagePrompt: A symbolic wall of light stretching across the galaxy, civilizations as tiny glowing dots unable to pass through, conceptual sci-fi art

lessons.title: What silence sounds like
lessons.body: Since 1960, projects like SETI have scanned billions of stars for signals. We've found pulsars, fast radio bursts, and strange dimming stars — but no signature that's unambiguously artificial. Even with Breakthrough Listen surveying a million nearby stars, the sky so far is cosmically quiet in every direction we point a dish.

visual.imagePrompt: A massive radio telescope dish at twilight under a star-dense sky, cold blue tones, sense of listening into an enormous emptiness

lessons.title: Why the silence matters
lessons.body: The Fermi Paradox isn't really about aliens — it's about us. Every proposed answer, from 'we're the first' to 'they're hiding' to 'they all blew themselves up,' tells us something about the fragility and strangeness of being a thinking species. The sky's silence is either the loneliest fact in the universe, or the biggest clue we've ever been handed.

quiz.question: Who first posed the question that became the Fermi Paradox?
quiz.explanation: Fermi asked 'Where is everybody?' during a casual lunch conversation at Los Alamos in 1950.

quiz.question: What does the 'Great Filter' hypothesis propose?
options: A barrier somewhere on the path from chemistry to interstellar civilization blocks almost all life
Light pollution on Earth hides alien signals
quiz.explanation: The Great Filter proposes some step in the evolution of intelligent life is so hard that almost nothing makes it past.

quiz.question: Why is the Milky Way's size a puzzle for the paradox?
options: It's too small to contain aliens
Even at slow speeds, a single civilization could colonize the galaxy in a few million years — far less than its age
It's expanding too fast for signals to reach us
Its center is too radioactive for life
quiz.explanation: The galaxy is ~13 billion years old; a spreading civilization would need only a few million years to be everywhere.

sources.title: Where Is Everybody? Fifty Solutions to the Fermi Paradox
The Great Filter - Are We Almost Past It?
